summary: DeVault argues that software complexity has become an epidemic, with modern tools like Gulp, Docker, and Chef adding massive complexity costs that outweigh their value. He contends these tools exist because developers haven't bothered learning simpler, venerable alternatives like Make, shell scripts, and package managers. The problem extends beyond tools to languages like C++ and Rust, bloated desktop apps shipping entire browsers, and websites laden with JavaScript and spyware. With Moore's law running out, he calls on developers to prioritize simplicity and dismantle the fragile abstractions that have accumulated.
key_points: Modern dev tools like Gulp, Docker, and Chef are often substitutes for not learning simpler existing tools like Make, shell scripts, and package managers
Dependencies are fallible — never add one you wouldn't feel comfortable debugging, because the complexity cost shows itself when systems inevitably break
Reinventing the wheel is fine, but many complicated tools are built by people who didn't fully understand what they were replacing
The complexity epidemic extends to programming languages, desktop apps shipping web browsers, server apps shipping entire OSes, and bloated web pages
With Moore's law ending, the free lunch of throwing hardware at bad software is over
money_quotes: Complexity is quickly becoming an epidemic. In this developer's opinion, complexity is the ultimate enemy - the final boss - of good software design.
Complicated software generally has complicated bugs. Simple software generally has simple bugs. It's as easy as that.
Don't forget that dependencies are fallible, and never add a dependency you wouldn't feel comfortable debugging.
The problem isn't in reinventing the wheel - it's in doing so before you actually understand the wheel.
Desktop applications are shipping entire web browsers into your disk and your RAM; server applications ship entire operating systems in glorified chroots.
This is an epidemic. It's time we cut this shit out.
Moore's law is running out, the free lunch is coming to an end. We have heaps and heaps of complicated, fragile abstractions to dismantle.
key_insight: The value added by modern development tools rarely outweighs the complexity cost they impose, and developers should learn simpler, proven tools rather than piling on fragile abstractions.
